Supporting Material: Environmental Chemicals as Modifiers of the Association between Age and Antral Follicle Count

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source
Author
Affiliation

Ashley I Naimi

Emory University

Published

July 14, 2026

1 Introduction

This report presents the complete analysis pipeline for examining whether and how environmental endocrine disrupting chemicals modify the association between age and antral follicle count (AFC) in women from the EARTH Study.

Study Population: 775 women aged 21-46 years

Methods: - DR Learner (doubly robust learner with SuperLearner)

2 Step 0: Missing Data Analysis and Imputation

This section summarizes the missing data patterns in the raw EARTH Study data and describes the random forest imputation approach used to handle missingness before analysis.

2.1 Missing Data Summary

Code 
missing_summary <- readRDS(here("output", "missing_data_summary.rds"))
knitr::kable(missing_summary, caption = "Missing Data Summary by Variable")
Missing Data Summary by Variable
variable n_missing pct_missing n_complete
BPF 666 85.94 109
ipPPP 610 78.71 165
BDCIPP 608 78.45 167
DPHP 594 76.65 181
sgratio_opfr 579 74.71 196
BPS 503 64.90 272
BP_3 318 41.03 457
TCS 318 41.03 457
mCNP 138 17.81 637
mCOP 138 17.81 637
B_PB 117 15.10 658
M_PB 117 15.10 658
P_PB 117 15.10 658
sgratio_pht 92 11.87 683
sgratio_bpa 92 11.87 683
mBP 92 11.87 683
miBP 92 11.87 683
mECPP 92 11.87 683
mEHHP 92 11.87 683
mEHP 92 11.87 683
mEOHP 92 11.87 683
mCPP 92 11.87 683
mEP1 92 11.87 683
mBZP1 92 11.87 683
dehp 92 11.87 683
BPA 92 11.87 683
Hg 84 10.84 691
previousIUI 55 7.10 720
previousIVF 55 7.10 720
bmi 12 1.55 763
smokstat 5 0.65 770
races 4 0.52 771
age 4 0.52 771
AFScanDate 2 0.26 773
educ1 1 0.13 774
gravid 1 0.13 774
id 0 0.00 775
AFC_t 0 0.00 775
sartnew2 0 0.00 775

This table shows the extent of missing data for each variable in the raw dataset. Variables with more than 40% missingness were excluded from the analysis (including BPS, BPF, BP3, TCS, and all OPFR metabolites).

2.2 Missing Data Visualization

This bar chart visualizes the percentage of missing values for each variable, making it easy to identify which variables have substantial missingness.

2.3 Distribution Comparison: Original vs Imputed

This multi-panel figure compares the distributions of observed data (blue, solid line) versus complete imputed data (red, solid line) for the 12 variables with the most missing values. The similarity between distributions suggests that the random forest imputation preserved the underlying data structure.

2.4 Imputation Performance

Code 
imputation_error <- readRDS(here("output", "imputation_error.rds"))
knitr::kable(imputation_error, caption = "Random Forest Imputation Out-of-Bag Error Estimates")
Random Forest Imputation Out-of-Bag Error Estimates
NRMSE PFC
NRMSE 0.47 0.33

The out-of-bag (OOB) error estimates provide measures of imputation accuracy. NRMSE (normalized root mean squared error) applies to continuous variables, while PFC (proportion falsely classified) applies to categorical variables. Lower values indicate better imputation performance.

2.5 Temporal Relationship Between Measurements

This section examines the timing between environmental exposure measurements and AFC assessment to understand the temporal structure of the data.

Panel A shows the distribution of time (in weeks) between when phthalate and BPA samples were collected and when AFC was measured. Panel B shows the distribution for mercury samples. The density overlays help visualize the central tendency and spread of the measurement timing. Comparing the two panels reveals whether different exposure biomarkers were collected on different schedules relative to the AFC assessment.

2.5.1 Summary Statistics for Measurement Timing

Code 
date_summary <- readRDS(here("output", "date_comparison_summary.rds"))
knitr::kable(date_summary, caption = "Summary Statistics for Time Between Exposure Measurement and AFC Assessment")
Summary Statistics for Time Between Exposure Measurement and AFC Assessment
Variable Min 1st Qu Median Mean 3rd Qu Max
PHT/BPA to AFC (weeks) -124 0.6 4.0 8.2 12 129
Hg to AFC (weeks) -140 0.0 4.4 6.9 15 201

This table provides summary statistics for the temporal gaps between exposure biomarker collection and AFC measurement. These timing patterns are important for understanding the exposure window being assessed relative to the outcome measurement.

2.5.2 Sample Timing Relative to the AFC Scan

Because EARTH participants provided urine and hair samples at study entry and during subsequent treatment cycles, the single sample selected as closest to the AFC assessment could be collected either before or after the scan. The table below reports how many exposure samples fell on each side of the AFC scan.

Code 
date_timing_counts <- readRDS(here("output", "date_timing_counts.rds"))
date_timing_display <- data.frame(
  Variable = date_timing_counts$Variable,
  N = date_timing_counts$N,
  `Collected after AFC scan, n (%)`  = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", date_timing_counts$n_after,  date_timing_counts$pct_after),
  `Collected before AFC scan, n (%)` = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", date_timing_counts$n_before, date_timing_counts$pct_before),
  `Same day, n (%)`                  = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", date_timing_counts$n_same,   date_timing_counts$pct_same),
  check.names = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(
  date_timing_display,
  caption = "Number of exposure samples collected before, after, or on the same day as the AFC scan. A sample is classified as collected *after* the scan when its collection date is later than the AFC scan date (equivalently, a negative value of AFC scan date minus sample date)."
)
Number of exposure samples collected before, after, or on the same day as the AFC scan. A sample is classified as collected after the scan when its collection date is later than the AFC scan date (equivalently, a negative value of AFC scan date minus sample date).
Variable N Collected after AFC scan, n (%) Collected before AFC scan, n (%) Same day, n (%)
PHT/BPA 683 549 (80.4%) 93 (13.6%) 41 (6.0%)
Hg 700 502 (71.7%) 165 (23.6%) 33 (4.7%)

For most participants, the exposure biomarker was measured after the AFC scan: 549 of 683 phthalate/BPA samples (80.4%) and 502 of 700 hair mercury samples (71.7%) were collected after the scan, compared with 13.6% and 23.6% collected before it. This confirms that the closest available sample could fall on either side of the AFC assessment and, in the large majority of cases, was measured shortly afterward.

3 Step 1: Data Management and Preprocessing

This section presents summary statistics from the initial data management and preprocessing stage. We examine the distribution of antral follicle count (AFC) by diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) status, overall data quality metrics, and key summary statistics for our analysis variables.

3.1 AFC Summary by DOR Status

Code 
afc_summary <- readRDS(here("output", "afc_summary_by_DOR.rds"))
knitr::kable(afc_summary, caption = "Summary of AFC by DOR Status")
Summary of AFC by DOR Status
DOR n() min(AFCt) max(AFCt) mean(AFCt) median(AFCt)
0 686 2 60 14.8 13
1 89 1 24 6.8 6

This table summarizes the antral follicle count stratified by diminished ovarian reserve status. What’s initially unclear here is the distinction between DOR and AFC? Why do women with no DOR have AFCs as low as 2?

4 Step 2: Chem Analysis

This section examines the distribution and characteristics of environmental chemical exposures measured in the EARTH Study cohort. We visualize the exposure distributions, assess the impact of truncating extreme values, and conduct comprehensive outlier analysis to identify observations with unusual chemical exposure patterns.

4.1 Environmental Variables Distribution (Non-Truncated)

This figure displays the raw distributions of all measured environmental chemical exposures without any truncation of extreme values.

4.2 Environmental Variables Distribution (Truncated)

This figure shows the distributions of chemical exposures after truncating extreme values to reduce the influence of outliers.

4.3 Outlier Analysis

This subsection presents a detailed examination of multivariate outliers across the chemical exposure space to identify individuals with unusual exposure profiles.

4.3.1 Outlier Detection Grid

This grid visualization displays multiple outlier detection metrics applied to each chemical exposure variable individually. We looked at Mahalanobis distances, principal components using single value decompositions, the local outlier factor, and Cook’s D.

4.3.2 Outlier Heatmap

The heatmap provides a multivariate perspective on outlier patterns, showing which individuals have unusual combinations of chemical exposures based on all of the outlier detection methods used. For example, ID 70 was deemed an outlier according to all methods, but the majority of outliers were deemed as such only by two or three methods.

4.3.3 Identified Outliers

Code 
outliers_id <- readRDS(here("output", "outliers_id.rds"))
Warning in gzfile(file, "rb"): cannot open compressed file
'/Users/ain/Dropbox/01 Projects/100. Under
Review/EARTH_Analysis_DR/output/outliers_id.rds', probable reason 'No such file
or directory'
Error in `gzfile()`:
! cannot open the connection
Code 
knitr::kable(outliers_id, caption = "Summary of Identified Outlier Observations")
Error:
! object 'outliers_id' not found

This table summarizes the observations identified as outliers through the various detection methods.

5 Step 3: Influence Function Score Generation

This section presents results from fitting the algorithms needed for the doubly robust learner. These algorithms include fits for the outcome regression (mu) and propensity score (pi) models using SuperLearner. The influence function scores are then used to construct AIPW scores, which can be averaged to compute the conditional associations and marginal associations defined in the main paper.

5.1 Main Scatterplot: AFC vs Age

This scatterplot visualizes the relationship between age (treatment variable) and antral follicle count (outcome) in the study population. The plot provides a visual assessment of the strength and form of the age-AFC association before adjustment for confounders.

5.2 Descriptive Statistics (Table 1)

Code 
descriptives_table1 <- readRDS(here("output", "descriptives_table1.rds"))
knitr::kable(descriptives_table1, caption = "Descriptive Statistics of Analysis Sample")
Descriptive Statistics of Analysis Sample
as.numeric(age >= 35) mean_bmi mean_gravid mean_IUI mean_IVF mean_AFC
0 24 0.34 0.25 0.09 17
1 25 0.54 0.32 0.19 12

This table presents baseline characteristics of the analysis sample, typically stratified by age groups or other relevant factors.

5.3 SuperLearner Outcome Model (Mu) Summary

mu_sl_summary <- readRDS(here("output", "mu_sl_summary.rds"))
print(mu_sl_summary$Table)

21 SL.glm.interaction_screen.corP  63 4.4  38  78

This output summarizes the performance of the SuperLearner ensemble used to estimate the outcome regression function (conditional mean of AFC given covariates). The summary includes algorithm weights and cross-validated performance metrics (cross-validated mean squared error), demonstrating how well the ensemble predicts AFC.

21 SL.glm.interaction_screen.corP 0.26 0.0082 0.22 0.37

This output summarizes the SuperLearner ensemble used to estimate the generalized propensity score (conditional density of age given covariates). The summary shows which algorithms contribute most to predicting age and the overall cross-validated performance (cross-validated mean squared error).

5.5 Variable Importance Plot

This figure displays the relative importance of covariates in predicting antral follicle count, derived from the SuperLearner ensembles. Variable importance measure used specifically is the permutation method.

5.6 Propensity Score Overlap

This plot shows the propensity score overlap from the SuperLearner approach, displaying the density of predicted propensity scores for treatment (age ≥ 35) and control (age < 35) groups.

5.7 Average Treatment Effect (ATE) Results

Code 
res_ate <- readRDS(here("output", "ate_results.rds"))
knitr::kable(res_ate, caption = "Average Treatment Effect Estimates")
Average Treatment Effect Estimates
estimate std.err LCL UCL
AIPW with Stacking -5 0.6 -6.2 -3.9

This table presents the estimated marginally adjusted association between age and AFC. This association quantifies the average AFC among women ≥ 35 years, versus women < 35 years, standardized to the distribution of confounders and covariates included in the outcome model. This overall association serves as a baseline for comparison when examining how the association varies across levels of chemical exposures in the heterogeneity analysis.

6 Step 4: CATE Functions

This section presents the main heterogeneity analysis results, examining whether and how environmental chemical exposures modify the age-AFC relationship. We use best linear projection to test for heterogeneity and visualize conditional average treatment effect (CATE) functions that show how the age effect varies across chemical exposure levels.

6.1 Linear Projection Results (Table 2)

Code 
table2 <- readRDS(here("output", "linear_projection_output.rds"))
knitr::kable(table2, caption = "Best Linear Projection: Tests for Treatment Effect Heterogeneity")
Best Linear Projection: Tests for Treatment Effect Heterogeneity
Type term estimate std.error statistic p.value
unconditional MBP -0.04 0.56 -0.06 0.95
unconditional MiBP -0.94 0.59 -1.60 0.11
unconditional MCNP -0.13 0.46 -0.29 0.77
unconditional MCOP -0.70 0.40 -1.74 0.08
unconditional MECPP -0.04 0.52 -0.07 0.94
unconditional MEHHP 0.00 0.49 -0.01 0.99
unconditional MEHP -0.45 0.44 -1.02 0.31
unconditional MEOHP -0.04 0.48 -0.08 0.93
unconditional MCPP -0.43 0.45 -0.96 0.34
unconditional MEP -0.32 0.45 -0.71 0.48
unconditional MBzP -0.52 0.53 -1.00 0.32
unconditional BPA 0.36 0.62 0.57 0.57
unconditional BP 0.26 0.30 0.86 0.39
unconditional MP -0.27 0.38 -0.72 0.47
unconditional PP -0.29 0.29 -0.99 0.32
unconditional Hg 1.04 0.49 2.10 0.04
conditional MBP 1.48 0.96 1.54 0.13
conditional MiBP -1.49 0.89 -1.67 0.10
conditional MCNP 0.36 0.69 0.52 0.60
conditional MCOP -0.91 0.64 -1.42 0.16
conditional MECPP 0.42 1.57 0.27 0.79
conditional MEHHP 0.39 2.71 0.14 0.89
conditional MEHP -1.24 0.76 -1.62 0.11
conditional MEOHP -0.09 2.92 -0.03 0.97
conditional MCPP 0.23 0.67 0.35 0.73
conditional MEP -0.30 0.54 -0.56 0.58
conditional MBzP -0.52 0.69 -0.76 0.45
conditional BPA 0.56 0.67 0.83 0.40
conditional BP 0.52 0.37 1.40 0.16
conditional MP -0.01 0.85 -0.01 0.99
conditional PP -0.40 0.66 -0.60 0.55
conditional Hg 0.94 0.51 1.83 0.07

This table presents the heterogeneity analysis by projecting the estimated CATEs onto each environmental chemical exposure. Large and precise coefficients indicate that the age-AFC association varies as a function of the chemical exposure level. The best linear projection approach provides valid inference even when the CATE function is not truly linear, making it a robust tool for detecting effect modification, provided that one understands one is interpreting a projection model, and not a true model.

6.2 Heterogeneity Test Statistics

This forest plot visualizes the test statistics from the best linear projection analysis, showing the strength of evidence for effect modification by each chemical exposure. Solid dots represent unconditional models (one EDC at a time), while plus signs represent conditional models (all EDCs together). Chemicals are ordered by test statistic magnitude, with reference lines indicating different significance thresholds. The plot allows quick identification of which chemicals show the strongest evidence for effect modification.

6.3 CATE Functions by Chemical Exposure

6.3.1 Linear CATE Functions (All Chemicals)

This multi-panel figure displays the estimated CATE functions using simple linear models for all 17 environmental chemicals. Each panel shows how the age-AFC association (y-axis) changes linearly across the log-transformed distribution of each chemical exposure (x-axis). The horizontal dashed line represents the overall average treatment effect.

6.3.2 SuperLearner CATE Functions (Selected Chemicals)

This figure shows the CATE functions estimated using SuperLearner ensemble methods for selected chemicals of interest (MCOP and MiBP). The SuperLearner approach automatically selects the optimal smoothing method from a library of candidates (LOESS with varying span parameters for MCOP, and GAM with varying degrees for MiBP), providing data-adaptive flexible estimation of potentially non-linear relationships. The black line represents the estimated CATE function, with the rug plot at the bottom showing the distribution of observed exposure values.

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# Set working directory to project root
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = here::here())
options(knitr.kable.NA = '', digits = 2)
# Load required packages
library(here)
library(tidyverse)
```

# Introduction

This report presents the complete analysis pipeline for examining whether and how environmental endocrine disrupting chemicals modify the association between age and antral follicle count (AFC) in women from the EARTH Study.

**Study Population:** 775 women aged 21-46 years

**Methods:**
- DR Learner (doubly robust learner with SuperLearner)

---

# Step 0: Missing Data Analysis and Imputation

This section summarizes the missing data patterns in the raw EARTH Study data and describes the random forest imputation approach used to handle missingness before analysis.

## Missing Data Summary

```{r missing-summary}
missing_summary <- readRDS(here("output", "missing_data_summary.rds"))
knitr::kable(missing_summary, caption = "Missing Data Summary by Variable")
```

This table shows the extent of missing data for each variable in the raw dataset. Variables with more than 40% missingness were excluded from the analysis (including BPS, BPF, BP3, TCS, and all OPFR metabolites).

## Missing Data Visualization

![Missing data patterns by variable](../figures/missing_by_variable.png)

This bar chart visualizes the percentage of missing values for each variable, making it easy to identify which variables have substantial missingness.

## Distribution Comparison: Original vs Imputed

![Comparison of distributions before and after imputation](../figures/imputation_comparison.png)

This multi-panel figure compares the distributions of observed data (blue, solid line) versus complete imputed data (red, solid line) for the 12 variables with the most missing values. The similarity between distributions suggests that the random forest imputation preserved the underlying data structure.

## Imputation Performance

```{r imputation-error}
imputation_error <- readRDS(here("output", "imputation_error.rds"))
knitr::kable(imputation_error, caption = "Random Forest Imputation Out-of-Bag Error Estimates")
```

The out-of-bag (OOB) error estimates provide measures of imputation accuracy. NRMSE (normalized root mean squared error) applies to continuous variables, while PFC (proportion falsely classified) applies to categorical variables. Lower values indicate better imputation performance.

## Temporal Relationship Between Measurements

This section examines the timing between environmental exposure measurements and AFC assessment to understand the temporal structure of the data.

![Distribution of time lag between exposure measurements and AFC scan](../figures/date_diff_combined.png)

Panel A shows the distribution of time (in weeks) between when phthalate and BPA samples were collected and when AFC was measured. Panel B shows the distribution for mercury samples. The density overlays help visualize the central tendency and spread of the measurement timing. Comparing the two panels reveals whether different exposure biomarkers were collected on different schedules relative to the AFC assessment.

### Summary Statistics for Measurement Timing

```{r date-summary}
date_summary <- readRDS(here("output", "date_comparison_summary.rds"))
knitr::kable(date_summary, caption = "Summary Statistics for Time Between Exposure Measurement and AFC Assessment")
```

This table provides summary statistics for the temporal gaps between exposure biomarker collection and AFC measurement. These timing patterns are important for understanding the exposure window being assessed relative to the outcome measurement.

### Sample Timing Relative to the AFC Scan

Because EARTH participants provided urine and hair samples at study entry and during subsequent treatment cycles, the single sample selected as closest to the AFC assessment could be collected either before or after the scan. The table below reports how many exposure samples fell on each side of the AFC scan.

```{r date-timing-counts}
date_timing_counts <- readRDS(here("output", "date_timing_counts.rds"))
date_timing_display <- data.frame(
  Variable = date_timing_counts$Variable,
  N = date_timing_counts$N,
  `Collected after AFC scan, n (%)`  = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", date_timing_counts$n_after,  date_timing_counts$pct_after),
  `Collected before AFC scan, n (%)` = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", date_timing_counts$n_before, date_timing_counts$pct_before),
  `Same day, n (%)`                  = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", date_timing_counts$n_same,   date_timing_counts$pct_same),
  check.names = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(
  date_timing_display,
  caption = "Number of exposure samples collected before, after, or on the same day as the AFC scan. A sample is classified as collected *after* the scan when its collection date is later than the AFC scan date (equivalently, a negative value of AFC scan date minus sample date)."
)
```

For most participants, the exposure biomarker was measured *after* the AFC scan: `r date_timing_counts$n_after[1]` of `r date_timing_counts$N[1]` phthalate/BPA samples (`r round(date_timing_counts$pct_after[1], 1)`%) and `r date_timing_counts$n_after[2]` of `r date_timing_counts$N[2]` hair mercury samples (`r round(date_timing_counts$pct_after[2], 1)`%) were collected after the scan, compared with `r round(date_timing_counts$pct_before[1], 1)`% and `r round(date_timing_counts$pct_before[2], 1)`% collected before it. This confirms that the closest available sample could fall on either side of the AFC assessment and, in the large majority of cases, was measured shortly afterward.

---

# Step 1: Data Management and Preprocessing

This section presents summary statistics from the initial data management and preprocessing stage. We examine the distribution of antral follicle count (AFC) by diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) status, overall data quality metrics, and key summary statistics for our analysis variables.

## AFC Summary by DOR Status

```{r afc-summary}
afc_summary <- readRDS(here("output", "afc_summary_by_DOR.rds"))
knitr::kable(afc_summary, caption = "Summary of AFC by DOR Status")
```

This table summarizes the antral follicle count stratified by diminished ovarian reserve status. What's initially unclear here is the distinction between DOR and AFC? Why do women with no DOR have AFCs as low as 2?

## Data Quality Assessment

```{r skim-results}
skim_results <- readRDS(here("output", "skim_results.rds"))
skim_results[,-1]
```

The skimmed dataset provides a comprehensive overview of data completeness, distributions, and potential quality issues. 

# Step 2: Chem Analysis

This section examines the distribution and characteristics of environmental chemical exposures measured in the EARTH Study cohort. We visualize the exposure distributions, assess the impact of truncating extreme values, and conduct comprehensive outlier analysis to identify observations with unusual chemical exposure patterns.

## Environmental Variables Distribution (Non-Truncated)

![Distribution of environmental chemical exposures without truncation](../figures/env_vars_notrunc.png)

This figure displays the raw distributions of all measured environmental chemical exposures without any truncation of extreme values. 

## Environmental Variables Distribution (Truncated)

![Distribution of environmental chemical exposures with truncation](../figures/env_vars_trunc.png)

This figure shows the distributions of chemical exposures **after** truncating extreme values to reduce the influence of outliers. 

## Outlier Analysis

This subsection presents a detailed examination of multivariate outliers across the chemical exposure space to identify individuals with unusual exposure profiles.

### Outlier Detection Grid

![Grid of outlier detection metrics across chemical exposures](../figures/outlier_grid.png)

This grid visualization displays multiple outlier detection metrics applied to each chemical exposure variable individually. We looked at Mahalanobis distances, principal components using single value decompositions, the local outlier factor, and Cook's D.

### Outlier Heatmap

![Heatmap of multivariate outlier patterns](../figures/outlier_heatmap.png)

The heatmap provides a multivariate perspective on outlier patterns, showing which individuals have unusual combinations of chemical exposures based on all of the outlier detection methods used. For example, ID 70 was deemed an outlier according to all methods, but the majority of outliers were deemed as such only by two or three methods.

### Identified Outliers

```{r outlier-ids}
outliers_id <- readRDS(here("output", "outliers_id.rds"))
knitr::kable(outliers_id, caption = "Summary of Identified Outlier Observations")
```

This table summarizes the observations identified as outliers through the various detection methods.

# Step 3: Influence Function Score Generation

This section presents results from fitting the algorithms needed for the doubly robust learner. These algorithms include fits for the outcome regression (mu) and propensity score (pi) models using SuperLearner. The influence function scores are then used to construct AIPW scores, which can be averaged to compute the conditional associations and marginal associations defined in the main paper.

## Main Scatterplot: AFC vs Age

![Scatterplot of AFC against age](../figures/main_scatterplot.png)

This scatterplot visualizes the relationship between age (treatment variable) and antral follicle count (outcome) in the study population. The plot provides a visual assessment of the strength and form of the age-AFC association before adjustment for confounders.

## Descriptive Statistics (Table 1)

```{r descriptives-table1}
descriptives_table1 <- readRDS(here("output", "descriptives_table1.rds"))
knitr::kable(descriptives_table1, caption = "Descriptive Statistics of Analysis Sample")
```

This table presents baseline characteristics of the analysis sample, typically stratified by age groups or other relevant factors. 

## SuperLearner Outcome Model (Mu) Summary

```{r mu-sl-summary}
mu_sl_summary <- readRDS(here("output", "mu_sl_summary.rds"))
print(mu_sl_summary$Table)
```

This output summarizes the performance of the SuperLearner ensemble used to estimate the outcome regression function (conditional mean of AFC given covariates). The summary includes algorithm weights and cross-validated performance metrics (cross-validated mean squared error), demonstrating how well the ensemble predicts AFC. 

## SuperLearner Propensity Score (Pi) Summary

```{r pi-sl-summary}
pi_sl_summary <- readRDS(here("output", "pi_sl_summary.rds"))
print(pi_sl_summary$Table)
```

This output summarizes the SuperLearner ensemble used to estimate the generalized propensity score (conditional density of age given covariates). The summary shows which algorithms contribute most to predicting age and the overall cross-validated performance (cross-validated mean squared error).

## Variable Importance Plot

![Variable importance from SuperLearner models](../figures/variable_importance_plot.png)

This figure displays the relative importance of covariates in predicting antral follicle count, derived from the SuperLearner ensembles. Variable importance measure used specifically is the permutation method.

## Propensity Score Overlap

![Assessment of propensity score overlap across age distribution](../figures/ps_overlap_plot.png)

This plot shows the propensity score overlap from the SuperLearner approach, displaying the density of predicted propensity scores for treatment (age ≥ 35) and control (age < 35) groups.

## Average Treatment Effect (ATE) Results

```{r ate-results}
res_ate <- readRDS(here("output", "ate_results.rds"))
knitr::kable(res_ate, caption = "Average Treatment Effect Estimates")
```

This table presents the estimated marginally adjusted association between age and AFC. This association quantifies the average AFC among women ≥ 35 years, versus women < 35 years, standardized to the distribution of confounders and covariates included in the outcome model. This overall association serves as a baseline for comparison when examining how the association varies across levels of chemical exposures in the heterogeneity analysis.

# Step 4: CATE Functions

This section presents the main heterogeneity analysis results, examining whether and how environmental chemical exposures modify the age-AFC relationship. We use best linear projection to test for heterogeneity and visualize conditional average treatment effect (CATE) functions that show how the age effect varies across chemical exposure levels.

## Linear Projection Results (Table 2)

```{r linear-projection}
table2 <- readRDS(here("output", "linear_projection_output.rds"))
knitr::kable(table2, caption = "Best Linear Projection: Tests for Treatment Effect Heterogeneity")
```

This table presents the heterogeneity analysis by projecting the estimated CATEs onto each environmental chemical exposure. Large and precise coefficients indicate that the age-AFC association varies as a function of the chemical exposure level. The best linear projection approach provides valid inference even when the CATE function is not truly linear, making it a robust tool for detecting effect modification, provided that one understands one is interpreting a projection model, and not a true model.

## Heterogeneity Test Statistics

![Forest plot of heterogeneity test statistics across chemical exposures](../figures/teststat_scatter.png)

This forest plot visualizes the test statistics from the best linear projection analysis, showing the strength of evidence for effect modification by each chemical exposure. Solid dots represent unconditional models (one EDC at a time), while plus signs represent conditional models (all EDCs together). Chemicals are ordered by test statistic magnitude, with reference lines indicating different significance thresholds. The plot allows quick identification of which chemicals show the strongest evidence for effect modification. 

## CATE Functions by Chemical Exposure

### Linear CATE Functions (All Chemicals)

![Linear CATE functions for all 17 environmental chemicals](../figures/cate_functions_full_linear.png)

This multi-panel figure displays the estimated CATE functions using simple linear models for all 17 environmental chemicals. Each panel shows how the age-AFC association (y-axis) changes linearly across the log-transformed distribution of each chemical exposure (x-axis). The horizontal dashed line represents the overall average treatment effect.

### SuperLearner CATE Functions (Selected Chemicals)

![SuperLearner-based CATE functions for selected chemicals](../figures/cate_functions_paper.png)

This figure shows the CATE functions estimated using SuperLearner ensemble methods for selected chemicals of interest (MCOP and MiBP). The SuperLearner approach automatically selects the optimal smoothing method from a library of candidates (LOESS with varying span parameters for MCOP, and GAM with varying degrees for MiBP), providing data-adaptive flexible estimation of potentially non-linear relationships. The black line represents the estimated CATE function, with the rug plot at the bottom showing the distribution of observed exposure values.
